content.assessment: A transparency and evidence-registry framework does not itself alter geographic access, capacity, or transport arrangements; from this lens its value is instrumental, providing the data infrastructure (settlement-level demographic trends, transport-responsibility gaps, cost realization) needed to evaluate any structural model's network feasibility, contingent on political will to use it.
opportunities: Could incorporate population distribution data (capital/town/village shares, migration reversal trends) directly into viability thresholds, making the registry geographically granular rather than purely fiscal
strengths: Directly proposes tracking realized cost savings and full transport cost offsets, addressing gaps identified in disputed transport-responsibility assignment and contested consolidation savings evidence

Explicitly commits to tracking Hungary-specific closure-population outcomes rather than assuming international findings (which are themselves contested across Finland, Denmark, Saxony, Sweden) transfer directly
threats: Delay risk is real: establishing evidence infrastructure takes time while the school-age cohort continues shrinking nationwide, meaning the network keeps changing during the framework's build-out
Registry could be rendered moot if centralized authorities continue to make closure decisions administratively without dedicated statutory triggers, as historically observed
weaknesses: An information framework does not by itself resolve any geographic, capacity, or transport constraint; it is a precondition for informed decisions rather than a network intervention

Historical pattern shows large-scale network contraction (from over 6,000 to nearly 4,200 schools) was driven by top-down political decisions independent of demographic-trigger rules, suggesting an evidence registry may be bypassed by similar future political decisions
